name: Oz by Warp
description: Oz is Warp's cloud-based orchestration platform for running, managing, and scaling coding agents. Launched February 2026, Oz lets individuals and teams spin up unbounded numbers of agents that write code across multiple repos, push commits, open PRs, run tests, and make fixes — all with full audit trails and controllable via CLI, API, and web dashboard. Multi-model by design (Claude, Codex, Gemini). Fully programmable with triggers, schedules, webhooks, and your own hosting.
oneLiner: Cloud orchestration platform for coding agents at scale — multi-model, multi-repo, fully programmable.
funding: $73M+ total ($50M Series B led by Sequoia Capital, June 2023). 700K+ active developers.
pricing: Free $0/mo; Build $18/mo; Max $180/mo; Business $45/user/mo; Enterprise custom. Oz agents priced on AI usage + compute. BYOK supported.
capabilities: Unbounded cloud agent scaling — spin up any number of agents
Multi-model support (Claude, Codex, Gemini) per agent
Multi-repo code changes, commits, PRs, test runs
Triggers — schedule, webhook, CI step, API call, manual
Full audit trail — every agent action tracked and linkable
CLI, REST API, SDK, and web dashboard interfaces
Team collaboration — share sessions, review actions, steer running agents
BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) for LLM providers
Warp cloud or self-hosted agent execution
Terminal-native — built on Warp's AI terminal product
Programmable orchestration — compose agent workflows via API

uxAnalysis.builderFlow: Terminal-native: agents launched from Warp terminal or via API/CLI. Web dashboard for monitoring and management. Triggers enable automated agent execution. Multi-model selection per agent task.
strengths: Terminal-first UX is natural for developers — no context switch
Multi-model per task enables cost-quality optimization automatically
Cloud execution means agents run without tying up developer machines
Full audit trail with shareable links per agent session
Programmable triggers integrate into existing CI/CD workflows
700K+ active developer base from Warp terminal product
weaknesses: No visual workflow builder — terminal/API only
Coding agents only — no general-purpose agent building
Closed source — no self-modification or framework extension
Warp terminal lock-in for the full experience
New product (Feb 2026) — production track record limited
Pricing complexity — AI usage + compute stacking
notableFeatures: Unbounded agent scaling — no cap on concurrent agents
Multi-repo support — agents work across multiple codebases simultaneously
Trigger system — schedule, webhook, CI, API, manual
Team steering — anyone on the team can review and redirect running agents
Terminal-Bench #1 ranking for Warp's AI terminal capabilities

recentDevelopments: 2026-02-10: Oz launched — cloud orchestration platform for coding agents
2026-02: New pricing with Build tier ($18/mo) and BYOK support
2026: 700K+ active developers on Warp terminal platform
2026: Multi-model support — Claude, Codex, Gemini available per agent

deploymentOptions.notes: Agents run on Warp's cloud or your own infrastructure. BYOK for LLM providers.

subCriteria.name: Agent Builder UX
subCriteria.rationale: Terminal/CLI/API-based agent creation. No visual builder. Fast for developers, inaccessible to business users.

subCriteria.name: Time-to-Value
subCriteria.rationale: Quick agent spin-up from terminal or API. Templates and triggers reduce setup time.

subCriteria.name: Debugging & Testing
subCriteria.rationale: Audit trails and shareable session links. No dedicated debugging UI beyond terminal output.

subCriteria.name: Documentation & Learning
subCriteria.rationale: 58-min live workshop, docs site, blog posts. Growing but still early.

subCriteria.name: Multi-Agent Coordination
subCriteria.rationale: Core strength — unbounded agents, multi-repo, parallel execution.

subCriteria.name: Human-in-the-Loop Design
subCriteria.rationale: Team steering — anyone can review and redirect running agents in real-time.

subCriteria.name: Workflow Templates
subCriteria.rationale: Trigger system with schedules, webhooks, CI integration. Not visual templates.

subCriteria.name: Team Development
subCriteria.rationale: Shared sessions, collaborative agent review, team-wide visibility.

subCriteria.name: Native Connectors
subCriteria.rationale: Git/GitHub integration native. CI/CD triggers. Limited beyond code toolchain.

subCriteria.name: API & Protocol Standards
subCriteria.rationale: REST API, SDK, CLI, webhooks. Well-designed programmable interface.

subCriteria.name: Authentication & Access
subCriteria.rationale: BYOK for LLM providers. Team auth via Warp accounts.

subCriteria.name: Data Pipeline & RAG
subCriteria.rationale: Multi-model routing built in. Agents access repos and file systems natively.

subCriteria.name: Guardrails & Kill Switches
subCriteria.rationale: Agent steering and termination available. No content guardrails like PII/moderation.

subCriteria.name: Audit Trail & Compliance
subCriteria.rationale: Full audit trail per agent session with shareable links. No compliance certifications yet.

subCriteria.name: RBAC & Access Control
subCriteria.rationale: Team-level access. No granular RBAC or enterprise SSO documented.

subCriteria.name: Explainability & Transparency
subCriteria.rationale: Agent actions visible in terminal/dashboard. No structured reasoning traces.

subCriteria.name: Deployment Flexibility
subCriteria.rationale: Warp cloud or self-hosted. BYOC support. Trigger-based deployment.

subCriteria.name: Scaling & Monitoring
subCriteria.rationale: Unbounded agent scaling. Dashboard monitoring. Limited observability depth.

subCriteria.name: Cost Management
subCriteria.rationale: Usage-based pricing but AI + compute stacking makes costs hard to predict.

subCriteria.name: Ecosystem Maturity
subCriteria.rationale: 700K developers from Warp terminal. Oz is new (Feb 2026) — ecosystem building.

scoringRationale: 64/100 overall. Strongest: Orchestration (15/20). Weakest: Governance (10/20). Breakdown: Creation (13/20), Orchestration (15/20), Integration (14/20), Governance (10/20), Operations (12/20).
targetUser: Engineering teams scaling coding agents across repositories

communityBuzz: Strong developer awareness from Warp terminal brand — 700K+ active developers
Oz positioned as the orchestration layer for 2026 agent explosion
172K views on intro video signals mainstream developer interest
Sequoia-backed with $73M+ funding — credible long-term bet
Early product (Feb 2026) — limited production feedback so far
marketIntelligence.funding: $73M+ total ($50M Series B led by Sequoia Capital, June 2023)

coreCompetence.bestAt: Cloud-native agent orchestration — unbounded scaling, multi-model, multi-repo, fully programmable triggers. The 'Kubernetes for coding agents.'
coreCompetence.targetUser: Engineering teams and platform engineers who need to run many coding agents at scale across repositories.
coreCompetence.vsAlternatives: Wins against CrewAI/LangGraph on cloud scaling and team collaboration. Loses to OpenAI Agent Builder on visual UX. Different category than Lindy/Vapi (code-only, not general purpose). Closest competitor: E2B (infrastructure) but Oz is higher-level (orchestration, not just sandboxing).

walkthrough.title: Oz by Warp: PM & CTO Walkthrough — Cloud Agent Orchestration

pmVerdict.summary: Oz fills the critical gap between 'one developer running one agent' and 'a team running hundreds of agents at scale.' The terminal-first UX is natural for developers but excludes everyone else. Multi-model per task, triggers, and team steering are genuinely differentiated. But it's coding-only — not a general-purpose agent factory. Best for: engineering orgs that have adopted AI coding agents and need orchestration infrastructure.
strengths: Only platform purpose-built for scaling coding agents in the cloud
Multi-model per task — Claude for reasoning, Codex for code, Gemini for review
Trigger system integrates agents into existing CI/CD workflows
Team steering lets anyone review and redirect running agents
700K developer base from Warp terminal gives instant distribution
risks: Coding agents only — not general-purpose agent building
No visual builder — terminal/API excludes non-developers
New product (Feb 2026) — limited production track record
Pricing complexity — AI + compute stacking makes costs unpredictable
Warp terminal dependency for the full experience

ctoVerdict.summary: Oz solves a real problem: as AI coding agents multiply, someone needs to orchestrate, monitor, and budget them. The audit trail, team steering, and trigger system are enterprise-grade thinking applied to a developer tool. But: no compliance certifications, limited RBAC, and the coding-only focus means it's a point solution, not a platform. Use it for your engineering team's agent fleet — not for customer-facing agents.
ctoVerdict.decision: EVALUATE — For Engineering Agent Fleet Management
strengths: Full audit trail per agent — every action tracked with shareable links
Self-hosted option — agents can run on your infrastructure
BYOK — bring your own API keys for any LLM provider
Sequoia-backed ($73M) — financially stable for a startup
dealBreakers: Coding agents only — can't orchestrate customer service, sales, or ops agents
No granular RBAC or SSO — team-level access only
Cost predictability is poor — stacked usage-based pricing
Feb 2026 launch — no production case studies yet

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Terminal-based agent creation shown in tutorials
scoringAlignment.gap: No visual builder — creation is CLI/API only
Orchestration is the product — well supported

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: API, CLI, webhooks, CI integration shown
scoringAlignment.gap: Integration beyond code tools not demonstrated

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Audit trails and session links demonstrated
scoringAlignment.gap: No compliance, RBAC, or content guardrails

scoringAlignment.videoEvidence: Cloud scaling, self-hosted option, dashboard monitoring shown

steps.title: Oz Architecture — Agent Orchestration Hub
steps.description: Oz sits at the center: Cron schedules, Web hooks, API/SDK calls, CLI, and Warp terminal all trigger agents. Multiple agents run in parallel — implementing features, searching bugs, building requests, cleaning backlogs.

works: Architecture diagram is instantly comprehensible — inputs on left, agents on right, Oz in the middle
Multiple trigger types (Cron, Web, API, CLI, Warp) cover every automation pattern
Parallel agents shown doing different tasks — not just one agent, a fleet
doesntWork: No visual builder — the architecture is conceptual, configuration is CLI/API
Agent task descriptions are vague (implementing features, searching bugs) — no detail on how
No error handling or fallback paths visible in the architecture
pmCritique.userPsychology: This diagram sells the vision: your engineering team augmented by an army of agents. The multiple input types signal this isnt a toy — its infrastructure.

ctoCritique.verdict: The architecture is sound — event-driven agent orchestration with multiple trigger sources. This is the Kubernetes model applied to AI agents.
concerns: No rate limiting or concurrency controls visible
Agent-to-agent coordination not shown — are they independent or can they collaborate?
Cost scaling with unbounded agents is a budget risk
enterpriseGaps: No priority queuing for agent tasks

steps.title: Cloud Agents Documentation
steps.description: Docs page explaining Cloud Agents: triggers (Slack, Linear, GitHub, webhooks), schedules (recurring tasks), parallelism (many agents across repos), and observability (tracked, auditable, shareable).

works: Clear documentation with use cases — PR reviews, issue triage, dead code removal
How they work together section explains local + cloud agent interplay
Seamless handoff — start in cloud, take over locally when hands-on needed
doesntWork: Docs-heavy onboarding — no interactive quickstart or playground
Multiple browser tabs open suggests complex setup workflow
pmCritique.userPsychology: The docs sell the value proposition clearly but the path from reading to doing requires switching to terminal. No in-browser try-it experience.

ctoCritique.verdict: Documentation quality is good — clear architecture explanation with practical use cases. Shared context via MCP servers is a thoughtful design choice.
concerns: Shared context between local and cloud agents raises data boundary questions
No security model documentation visible on this page
Integration list (Slack, Linear, GitHub) is limited compared to enterprise needs
enterpriseGaps: No data residency or compliance section
No SLA documentation for cloud agents

steps.title: Agent Running in Warp Terminal
steps.description: Warp terminal showing agent conversation with file listing, Run ID, Credits used (1.0), Run time (12.7 sec), Status (Failed), and Continue locally button for handoff.

works: Continue locally button enables seamless cloud-to-local handoff — unique feature
Run metadata (ID, credits, time, status) visible at a glance — no digging
Terminal UX is natural for developers — zero learning curve if you use Warp
doesntWork: Status shows Failed with no explanation — what went wrong?
1000 free cloud agent credits shown but credit consumption rate is opaque
Multiple tabs (agent conversations) can get overwhelming
pmCritique.userPsychology: The Continue locally button is the killer UX innovation — cloud agents do the heavy lifting, you take over for the tricky parts. This is genuine human-AI collaboration.

ctoCritique.verdict: Run metadata (ID, credits, time, status) is the right level of observability for individual agent runs. The failed status needs better error reporting.
concerns: Failed run with no error detail — debugging requires investigation
Credits as the billing unit is non-standard — hard to map to traditional cost models
Terminal-only interface limits who can monitor agent health
enterpriseGaps: No error categorization or automated retry
No alerting on failed agent runs
No cost forecasting based on credit usage patterns

steps.title: Agent Creates GitHub Pull Request
steps.description: @warp-agent opens PR #48 "Update footer year to 2026" — all checks pass (1 neutral, 4 successful), Cloudflare Pages deployed. Agent comments with code review suggestion.

works: Agent-created PR looks identical to human PR — @warp-agent is a team member
All checks pass including Cloudflare deployment — agent code is production-quality
Agent comments with specific suggestion (update starter range) — not just code, reviews too
doesntWork: Simple task (update year) doesnt demonstrate complex multi-file changes
No indication this was an agent PR without checking the author name
PR review by the agent on its own PR is a governance concern
pmCritique.userPsychology: Seeing a real GitHub PR created by an agent — with passing checks and deployment — is the proof point. This is what makes engineering managers say yes.

ctoCritique.verdict: Agent PRs with CI/CD integration is the right deployment model — agents work through the same process as humans. The governance question: who reviews agent PRs?
concerns: Agent self-reviewing its own PR is a conflict of interest
No mandatory human review gate before merge
Simple PR (year update) — complex multi-service changes need different safeguards
enterpriseGaps: No required human approval for agent PRs
No agent PR labeling or categorization
No agent change scope limits (max files, max lines)

steps.title: New Oz Agent Conversation
steps.description: Start screen for a new cloud agent: project context (~/Projects/bholmesdev), keyboard shortcuts, /model to switch LLMs, 972 free cloud credits remaining, Check agent status button.

works: Project context shown upfront — agent knows which codebase to work on
Keyboard shortcuts for power users (Cmd+Shift for cloud, Cmd for local)
/model switch makes LLM selection a first-class command
972 free credits — generous free tier for evaluation
doesntWork: Blank prompt with example text but no templates or suggested tasks
Credit counter creates cost anxiety — when will I run out?
No task history or recent agents visible from this screen
pmCritique.userPsychology: The free credits remove the purchase barrier. The keyboard shortcuts signal this is a power-user tool. But the blank prompt means the user must already know what to ask.

ctoCritique.verdict: Good developer UX — project context, model selection, and credit visibility in one screen. But no team management, no shared agent templates, no organizational controls.
concerns: Individual credit allocation — no team/org budget management
No approved model list — developers can use any model
No task templates for standardized agent workflows
enterpriseGaps: No team credit pools or chargeback
No shared agent task templates across team
